content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 24, 2000


The Honorable Martin Gallegos
California State Assembly
State Capitol, Room 6005
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-00-059

Dear Assemblymember Gallegos:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Are payments made at your behest for an awards breakfast "contributions"?

2. Are payments made at your behest for an awards breakfast "gifts"?

3. May your staff solicit sponsors for the breakfast?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. No. The payments made at your behest for the event are not "contributions" since the
breakfast is a governmental event. However, the payments may be reportable.

     2. The payments made at your behest for the event are not "gifts," unless the payments
confer a personal benefit to you. A payment of food and beverages that you consume confers a
personal benefit to you and is therefore a gift.

     3. The Act does not prohibit your staff from soliciting sponsors for the event.

_______________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18896, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-059
Page 2

FACTS

You will be sponsoring a Public Safety Appreciation Awards Breakfast at the Industry
Hills Sheraton in the City of Industry on May 6, 2000. The East Valley Boys and Girls Club will
also be sponsoring the event.

Your staff will contact each city in your district to identify police officers and firefighters
who deserve special recognition for exemplary service to their communities. Once identified,
these public safety workers will be honored at the awards breakfast.

Chambers of commerce and local businesses will sponsor the event through financial
contributions and donated items such as gift bags and food. The East Valley Boys and Girls
Club will be the sole agency responsible for receiving any contributions for the event.

As for publicity, newsletters from individual cities and chambers of commerce will
handle this portion of the event. In addition, you might seek the assistance of Member Services
in producing programs outlining the accomplishments of the honorees.

ANALYSIS

1. Are the payments contributions?

In general, a payment² made to, or at the behest of, a candidate is a "contribution" for
purposes of the Act. (Section 82015; Regulation 18215.) However, the term "contribution" has
many exceptions. For example, it exempts certain payments made at the behest of a candidate
for a legislative, governmental or charitable event:

"(2) A payment made at the behest of a candidate is a
contribution to the candidate unless ...
(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the
payment was made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy
for elective office. The following types of payments are presumed
to be for purposes unrelated to a candidate's candidacy for elective
office:
(iii) A payment ... made principally for legislative,
governmental, or charitable purposes." (Section 82015(b)(2)(B).)

² A "payment" means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of
money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.)

File No. A-00-059
Page 3

You will be sponsoring a breakfast honoring exemplary public safety personnel from
your district. The breakfast appears to be a governmental event. Therefore, any payment made
at your behest for the event is not a contribution.

2. Are the payments gifts?

A "gift" is any payment that confers a personal benefit to the extent that equal or greater
consideration is not received. (Section 82028(a).) Thus, a payment made at the behest of a
candidate for a legislative, governmental, or charitable purpose is not a gift, unless it confers a
personal benefit to the candidate. (Section 82015(b)(2)(b)(iii).)

The breakfast appears to be a governmental event. Therefore, any payment made at your
behest for the event is not a gift, unless it confers a personal benefit to you. A payment for food
and beverages confers a personal benefit on the person consuming the food or beverage.
Therefore, if you dine at the awards breakfast, the value of the food and beverages that you
consume will be considered a gift. (Regulation 18941.1.) If the value of the meal is $50 or
more, you must report the gift on your statement of economic interests. (Section 87207.)

3. May your staff solicit sponsors for the event?

The Act does not prohibit your staff from asking any person to coordinate the event,
provide financial support for the event, or donate items for the event.

4. What are your reporting obligations?

Even though they are not contributions, payments made at the behest of a candidate from
a single source for a legislative, governmental, or charitable purpose are reportable if the
payments equal $5,000 or more in a calendar year. (Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii).) You must
report these payments within 30 days. Once the $5,000 threshold from a single source has been
reached for a calendar year, all payments made by that source in the same calendar year must be
disclosed within 30 days.

5. Do any exceptions in Regulation 18215(c) apply?

Individual cities and chambers of commerce will be sending newsletters publicizing the
awards breakfast. In addition, you may seek the assistance of Members Services in producing
programs outlining the accomplishments of the honorees.

Regulation 18215(c) lists exceptions to the definition of contribution. One exception is a
communication that does not contain express advocacy:

File No. A-00-059
Page 4

"(c) [T]he term "contribution" does not include:
(4) A payment made at the behest of a candidate, which is for a
communication by the candidate or any other person, that meets all
of the following:
(i) Does not contain express advocacy;
(ii) Does not make reference to the candidate's candidacy for
elective office, the candidate's election campaign, or the
candidate's or his or her opponent's qualifications for office; and
(iii) Does not solicit contributions to the candidate or to third
persons for use in support of the candidate or in opposition to the
candidate's opponent." (Regulation 18215(c)(4).)

Accordingly, any communication distributed at your behest in connection with the
awards breakfast is not a contribution if the requirements in Regulation 18215(c)(4) are met.

6. Does the mass mailing prohibition apply?

Under Section 89001, a mass mailing may not be sent at public expense if all of the
following four criteria apply, and an exception in Regulation 18901(b) does not apply:

1. The item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place
of employment or business, or post office box; and the item is a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

2. The item sent either: (1) includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer, or (2) features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing.

3. Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or the costs of design,
production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with public moneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted.

4. More than 200 substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request. (Regulation 18901(a).)

It does not appear that the mass mailing prohibition will apply to the communications you
describe in your request. First, an announcement in a city's newsletter does not meet the second
requirement because, as a state legislator, you are not affiliated with a city. Second, it does not
appear that an announcement in a chamber of commerce newsletter will be paid for with public
funds. Finally, the programs for the event will not be mailed to anyone.

File No. A-00-059
Page 5

If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
